topic: A student learns their scholarship application was denied
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email sat on the desk, stark white against the dark wood. Elias stared at the screen, his breath catching in shallow, rapid bursts. Each line of the rejection felt like a physical blow, a cold, heavy weight settling in his chest. He reread the rejection letter for the third time, his eyes blurring slightly. The words swam, refusing to cohere into anything sensible. A tremor started in his hands, and he slowly pushed the chair back, the scraping sound loud in the quiet room. He walked to the window, looking out at the indifferent sprawl of the campus, feeling utterly hollowed out, as if the color had been leached from the world.

[story 2]

"No." The single word echoed in the small dorm room, bouncing off the bare walls. Maya sat on the edge of her bed, her fingers tracing the worn fabric of her sweatshirt. She kept looking at the notification pop-up on her laptop, a cruel little digital ghost. Her stomach twisted into a tight knot, making it hard to draw a full breath. She pressed her palms against her eyes, trying to physically block out the stinging sensation behind them. When she finally lowered her hands, her gaze fell to her hands, noticing the slight tremor in her fingers. She stood up abruptly, pacing the small space between her desk and the door, a frantic energy thrumming just beneath her skin.

[story 3]

He crumpled the application packet in his fist, the crisp paper tearing easily under the pressure. Leo didn't look at the screen; he just sat there, meticulously folding the paper into a tight, unnatural square. His jaw was clenched so tightly that a dull ache radiated through his temples. Every time he shifted his weight, he felt a profound sense of wrongness, a misalignment in the very structure of things. He picked up a pen, but the tip hovered over the paper, unwilling to commit to any further action. A slow, shallow sigh escaped him, a sound more like air escaping a punctured lung, as he stared blankly at the ceiling, unable to process the finality of the outcome.
